text: The surgeon injects a diluted solution of vasopressin into the posterior wall of the uterus, targeting the area around the nine-centimeter myoma, which extends into the cervix.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the serous layer of the uterus, which appears pale due to transient ischemia from vasopressin injection. A longitudinal incision is made to facilitate enucleation of the myoma.
The surgeon enlarges the incision on the uterus using a harmonic scalpel, following the initial longitudinal cut made for enucleation of the myoma.
The surgeon creates a relatively larger incision to facilitate the removal of the myoma, ensuring adequate stitching is performed post-removal.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw from the right hand side to facilitate the enucleation of the myoma, which has been cut from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon holds a traumatic grasper in the left hand while using a suction irrigation device, likely to clear the surgical field and manage fluids during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw and suction irrigation to facilitate the enucleation of the myoma, demonstrating the instruments' effectiveness in manipulating the tissue.
The surgeon assesses the surgical field, aiming to achieve a clear dissection plane for the removal of the myoma, which facilitates easier enucleation.
The surgeon uses two myoma screws to facilitate the enucleation of the myoma, providing additional traction and stability during the procedure.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to slowly separate the myoma from surrounding tissue, with visible progress in the dissection.
The surgeon encounters dense attachments near the base of the myoma, where feeding vessels are visible. Energy sources are used to separate these attachments.
The surgeon completes the separation of the final attachment of the myoma and proceeds with suturing and knotting to secure the tissue.
The surgeon uses a 40 mm needle to take deep bites through the muscle tissue, facilitating closure of the surgical site.
A small flap of the serous layer is visible, indicating a section of tissue that may require further manipulation or suturing.

The surgeon applies an extracorporeal knot to provide traction and tension, aiding in hemostasis. A needle holder is used to lock the knot, followed by the application of another extracorporeal square knot.
The surgeon ties the third knot, securing the suture in place during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon takes a deep bite with a 40 mm needle, incorporating the muscle layer to facilitate closure.
The surgeon ties a square knot, securing the suture in place within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the fourth suture, securing the tissue with precise needle placement.
The surgeon ties an extracorporeal knot, securing the suture outside the patient's body during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs morselation to remove the entire myoma from the uterine tissue.
